channel.title: Teacher Plus
channel.description: The magazine for the contemporary teacher

item.title: Pictures can re-make our world
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
We tend not to think of school textbooks as places where unconventional ideas can find expression. After all, textbooks are meant to contain sterile content, facts and concepts that have been examined and accepted, to be presented to children as a fair representation of the world they will grow up into. But lessons in textbooks can also be windows into the possible. They play a role in building a worldview, and fostering in the child an understanding of social and cultural norms. This is particularly true in primary school, where illustrations play an important role in explaining things to young children. This is why good publishers take special care with illustrations, ensuring that they convey information in positively stimulating ways.

So when the Kerala Government took a deliberate decision to populate textbooks of some classes with images and content that would challenge stereotypical gender roles, it was seen as an innovative move. The SCERT is reported to have said that these revisions have been made to reflect changes in society, and to foster empathy towards marginalized and under-represented groups, including but beyond gender.

In one widely shared image from a class 3 English textbook, a family is shown in their kitchen, with the mother standing at the stove while the father sits on the floor grating coconut while the two children look on. In another, the father is preparing a snack while a child looks on. A few other states, including Maharashtra, have also been doing this, pointing to a conscious effort on the part of educational governing bodies to do their bit to change gender norms.

The more cynical might argue that a few pictures in children’s textbooks cannot change the world, but the hopeful among us would say that such pictures can shift our expectations of life, simply by exposing us to what is possible. Visual representation in media of all kinds can make a difference to how we see ourselves and our place in the world. When girls see other girls taking on unconventional roles, they understand that they too can do these things. Similarly, a father in a textbook image can spark a conversation about a father in the home.

Of course, gender norms and other social and cultural rules are deep rooted; it will take many initiatives to dismantle them. But it’s also worth remembering that children spend many hours looking at their school books; for many, these are the only books they will have access to. The pictures in these books can normalize practices in both good and bad ways, and if the images show the kind of world we want to have, it’s possible that children will grow up expecting—and contributing to—such a vision.   

Usha Raman
item.excerpt:encoded: Textbooks aren't just about facts—they shape worldviews, writes editor Usha Raman. Depicting fathers cooking and mothers in diverse roles can help shift expectations and spark conversations. When children see inclusive representations, they learn that everyone can take on any role, she adds.

item.title: Finding your peace
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
I won’t deny it; the past month has been a tough one for those engaged with struggles over inequality and social justice. You might ask, which month—or week or day—is not?  That is true, but some months—days, weeks, hours—are harder than others. Teachers may not been seen as social justice warriors, but there’s no denying that our classrooms are places where we can glimpse the potential and possibility for social change, one individual at a time. Which is why—as the theme of this issue suggests—we need to find ways of finding and keeping our inner strength, ways of staying centered and calm even when the world around us seems to be falling apart.

If you pay any attention to the news, you would have seen the headlines screaming out at you about the horrific events involving a medical professional in Kolkata, or the equally horrible fate of a young woman outside Mumbai, or again, a teenager in Muzaffarpur, Bihar. Some of you may have joined protests or silently shed a tear, thinking that the stories never seem to end. Ace wrestler Vinesh Phogat remarked that while such gruesome incidents make the headlines, the struggle against gender based violence is an everyday fact in almost every sphere of activity. And, one might add, in almost every space, whether private or public, domestic or professional. The Justice Hema Committee report, made public in late July, which deals with gender-based exploitation in the Malayalam film industry, lists the persistent practices that render women vulnerable. It’s been called Kerala cinema’s “Me Too Moment”, leading us to wonder how many more industries and sectors will reveal their own sordid stories. The teaching community, and the education sector more broadly, are not exempt, and issues of gender based exploitation can affect those who deliver education, manage institutions, and, most crucially, those who enter these spaces to learn.

In Teacher Plus we do not often talk about such issues, except in the context of how we might make our classrooms more gender equitable, or how we can sensitize children in a way that makes them less likely to engage in gender-discriminatory behaviours. We have in the past delved deeply into matters of sexuality and sex education, carrying more than one series of articles that give teachers ideas about how to deal with these sensitive topics in class. But clearly what we are doing in our classrooms and in our homes is not enough to make this culture change. We still have to worry about our girls (and boys), and we still have to teach them ways of protecting themselves from a society that refuses to shift its ways of seeing and thinking.

But we must go on. And we must keep trying. And each time a young face looks at you with comprehension, or whose eyes light up with interest, or who sees the connections you have attempted to show, you know it’s all worth it. The ability to “keep on keeping on” comes from a space inside you fed by your own interests and passions, but it is also fed by these signs that tell you that what you’re doing touches lives. And maybe, can change things.

As for me, where do I go to find that energy, or to rest my mind and spirit when faced with the tragedies of the world, large and small? I fall into the comfort of a novel, to discover other worlds of possibility, and I cushion myself with music and poetry. When it’s not raining, and there’s a weekend with hours to spare, I take a walk in whatever piece of greenery I can find. Or I sit doing nothing, holding a cup of hot filter coffee.

Usha Raman
item.excerpt:encoded: Educators may not be on the front lines, but our classrooms are spaces where change begins. Editor Usha Raman writes about the role of teachers in fostering a more equitable world, even as we grapple with our own challenges.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Very true. Gender discrimination is very obvious in many of our schools. Violence against women teachers are not non-existent. School owners and other Administrators such as Correspondents seem to abuse women teachers. Apart from these, is classroom violence where students hit teachers. As far as I have read, more women teachers seem to be victims than men. These oddities should change. This needs a very strong teacher voice such as your wonderful magazine. Ofcourse, there are excellent child friendly, staeholder friendly and quality oriented schools; but these are like oasis in the desert. Thanks.

item.title: Structure, control, and flexibility
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
Usha Raman

No matter what the role, I suspect that most of us veer between a need for structure, a sense of order and clear boundaries, and the freedom of flexibility and space to move things around, to push those boundaries a bit, or even break them, and the excitement of disorder. It’s like the promise of an evening out at a familiar restaurant where we know we can experiment a bit in the certain knowledge that there will be the usual favourite dishes to come back to if we so desire. As teachers, this ambivalence – this co-existing desire for order and disorder – expresses itself in our syllabi and lesson plans, which offer a clear pathway to where we need to go with our teaching while also holding space for the minor adjustments that must be made for individual students and gives us some room to account for the mood of the group. We – and our students – are steadied by the presence of the map, yet can feel the power of holding the steering wheel as we manage the speed of the journey.

As someone who teaches at the university level, I may have a little more flexibility than the average school teacher in navigating that space between the structure of a course outline and its customized implementation in the classroom. In a recent class, for instance, I went in with my prepared presentation, and an activity planned out, with clear time allocation for the doing and the discussion. But five minutes into the class, a question threw us off course, and a discussion ensued that went in many fascinating directions, none of which had anything to do (ostensibly) with the planned lesson. If I had insisted on returning to my PowerPoint slides, or on shepherding the 50 students back into an activity that now seemed irrelevant, I would have lost the opportunity to let them explore a different set of ideas that seem to have captured their imagination. So, I let it go, and instead focused my energies on drawing from this unplanned discussion a different set of points that could be tied back to the larger goals of the course.

At the institutional level, the responsibility is to provide sufficient structure so that individuals can perform their roles with creativity and a degree of flexibility. This structure can be physical – walls around classrooms, or dividing different parts of the school – as well as conceptual – scheduling the day or the week. It can take the form of rules and regulations that mirror the expectations of society and state.

But even as we respect those boundaries, observe the rules, and cooperatively work by the regulations, it’s important that we find and keep the spaces we can for our own little digressions, our own interpretation of what is possible, and what is doable. Those are the spaces where innovation resides, and surprises emerge. And while we may return to the overarching structure routinely, and even feel comforted and anchored by it, we do so with a bit of bounce, an energy that comes from the unexpected flavour of experimentation.
item.excerpt:encoded: No matter what the role, I suspect that most of us veer between a need for structure, a sense of order and clear boundaries

item.title: The point of asking “what if?”
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
There are at least two ways in which we might imagine the future. One path takes us through unchecked climate change and its consequences on human and planetary life, including the exacerbation of conflicts around the world. The other path has us finding and implementing solutions to environmental collapse, with young people leading the way to a brighter, more sustainable future. The reality is perhaps going to be somewhere in between, but even to reach that halfway mark we need to believe in the possibility of a positive outcome.

One of the genres of writing that has always had a niche following but has gained popularity in recent times is speculative fiction – fiction that plays on the “what if?” question and departs from reality to imagine different worlds, some fantastical but others within the realm of possibility. Stories in this genre range from the dystopic and dark to those that are imbued with hope, seeking solutions to the many problems that beset the human race. The dark stories serve as a warning, cautionary tales that shake us out of our complacency and push us to act if we are to avoid such a future. The hopeful stories present us with alternative imaginations of what could be, showing us how we might get there. One of the sub-genres of speculative fiction (or ‘spec-fic”) is climate fiction, which lays out for us, in imaginative detail, what life could be like as climate change progresses. For many of us, the climate “catastrophe” (as it has been dubbed by scientists) is something in the future, or it is something beyond our capacity to deal with as individuals. It’s hard to think about whether and how we can actually do anything to change the direction we are moving in. Climate fiction portrays the consequences of catastrophe while also pointing us to possible responses, both as a society and as individuals. Storytelling, after all, is one of the most powerful ways of influencing how people think and getting them to imagine something different.

As teachers, it’s easy for us to become cynical and jaded, sometimes defeated by the circumstances in which we find ourselves. These may be immediate circumstances to do with one’s personal or professional life – from care responsibilities at home to poor infrastructure and institutional politics at work, from tedious commutes to challenging classrooms, and a host of other things. So how do we move past that to bring energy and purpose to our days? Possibly because the act of teaching demands hopeful speculation – we begin each year with the firm belief that we can change the futures of the children in our classroom, and we then proceed to showing them how they can move from a place of not knowing, to a place of understanding. And then we do it all over again, year after year.

That’s why we need hope. Even the darkest, most dystopian fiction is written in the hope that it can lead us to a different place, that nothing is inevitable, that maybe this will dislodge us from the conviction that nothing can change. It is after all, the season of lights, maybe the time of year when we are at our most hopeful!

We’d love to hear where your mind goes when allowed to speculate, to ask “what if…?”
item.excerpt:encoded: There are at least two ways in which we might imagine the future. One path takes us through unchecked climate change and its consequences on human and planetary life, including the exacerbation of conflicts around the world.

item.title: Small gestures with big impact
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
“It’s a gift,” says Jyoti Thyagarajan, long-time educator, currently NGO founder-director, and perennial adventurer. “What happens in the classroom is unpredictable, and it’s magic.”

Jo – as she prefers to be addressed – was in Hyderabad recently to flag off a project in Telangana government schools (more about this at some point in the future) that her Bangalore-based NGO, Meghshala, is implementing. Jo’s long career as a teacher of physics and mathematics has taken her across three countries and given her the opportunity to interact with a large number of students. She had many stories to tell, but this one stuck with me. A student she had taught several decades ago was visiting Bangalore and asked to meet. In conversation over lunch, he casually remarked that she had saved his life. Thinking he was just being overly dramatic in that moment of nostalgia, she insisted that she couldn’t have done any such thing. He recalled that one day, when he was feeling particularly low, overwhelmed by a sense that life just wasn’t working out, Jo had walked past him in the hallway and casually rested a hand on his shoulder. In that moment, he said, he knew that things would be okay because he wasn’t alone.

We had been discussing how much a teacher does, and can do, in ways that go so much beyond the imparting of a curriculum. And this opportunity to be an agent of change in a child’s life even with the smallest gesture is the “gift” that she was talking about. As it happened in this particular case with Jo’s student, this opportunity presents itself, often in ways invisible to us, practically every day. While this may be true of any occupation to varying extents, for those of us who work with children and young people, it’s what gives our work special meaning. And that impact often does not become apparent to us, if at all, until many years after the student leaves our classroom.

Of course, there are outcomes we expect in the short term – content understood, skills gained, and that sort of thing. But teachers do much more than just transmit content and build skills. The classroom, and by extension the school, is also a place where children learn how to be, to grow into themselves, and form relationships with each other and with the world they inhabit. The climate that a teacher builds in the classroom can feed into both curricular learning and social/emotional development, and when those work in tandem, that’s when the possibility of magic is created. This issue of Teacher Plus relates to quite a different kind of magic, however – that which is made possible by taking a design approach. This is a very special issue curated by design educators and designers Siddhi Gupta and Amitabh Kumar from the Srishti-Manipal Institute of Art, Design and Technology. Looking at design education broadly and through the experiences of those who have pursued design as a career, the curators have put together a package that will challenge and inspire us to think differently. We’d be curious to know how you respond to the articles in the issue.
item.excerpt:encoded: Teachers are magicians. The kind of classroom environment they create leads to not just curricular learning, but social and emotional development as well. This issue of Teacher Plus gives teachers the tool of design to create some more magic.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Respected Ms Usha Raman, I am a regular reader of Teacher Plus since early 1990s. I posted a comment on an article on Dr P K Srinivasan in your last month's edition. But even after nearly a month since then the comment section at the end of the article mentions "Your comment awaits moderation". How long is it going to be? I think that this is abnormal. Kindly fix if the delay. Thanks
We are sorry about the very irregular moderation of comments so far. We will be monitoring comments more regularly henceforth.

item.title: Engaging with conflict
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
Many of us may have grown up reading some of Mark Twain’s work – at least in abridged versions – and many of us may have taken delight in the stories, particularly in the rebellions of the central characters. Twain’s work has come under considerable criticism from many angles, but most notably for the use of racist terms and stereotypical depictions of Jim in The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn. Teachers in the United States have hesitated to teach the book or recommend it to their students. Teachers in India – particularly those who teach language or social studies – may have also experienced similar waves of approval and disapproval of texts and topics over the years, with some books moving out of our lists quietly while others are rudely excised by Boards, often without explanation. Whether one is teaching William Shakespeare or Munshi Premchand, there are likely to be things the authors say and describe that are quite different from the way we view the world today, including the use of language and terminology that we may no longer find acceptable or appropriate.

How do teachers deal with changing notions of “appropriate”? We live in an age where every cultural product is accompanied by trigger warnings and disclaimers, whether it is videos with flashing lights (which could disturb those with neurological conditions) or violent and sexual content (which could disturb children or others with specific sensitivities) or language (which could cause offense to certain groups). While some may find this all too much, arguing that we are being too protective, others would prefer that we are better safe than sorry. Some parents carefully curate their children’s viewing and reading exposure while others allow more freedom. Some would want schools to follow the same caution as they do, while others would want their children to have broader exposure to a diverse range of content.

I was speaking with a young teacher recently who mentioned that she sometimes gets complaints from parents objecting to something she showed in class, because it offended the parents’ religious sentiments, or because it was seen as promoting a certain kind of gender politics. In many cases, her school backs her decision, taking the position that exposure to sensitive content is always accompanied by explanation, contextualization, and nuanced discussion about what it means and how it could be interpreted. Even so, she remarked, it can get difficult to know how children – and parents – might react, so she tends to stick to a narrow set of content that is deemed safe: material that does not raise thorny political, religious, or cultural questions.

As someone who teaches young adults (and not very young children), I too am constantly debating about the material I bring into the classroom. Is it too liberal, or too conservative, too overtly political, or socially/culturally provocative? One would think that the purpose of education is to build the tools that allow young people to deal with the world in all its complexity, its beauty and its ugliness, its richness and its poverty, and to make up their own minds about the positions they wish to take. If we are too concerned with appropriateness in the moment, we will be foregoing the opportunity to engage with difficult debates, or worse, silence them altogether. Of course, there is the very valid concern with using age appropriate content, and we must cater to the maturity level of the students, but too often we underestimate the ability of students to deal with diverse materials. Our focus as educators should be to get students to think about what makes something appropriate or not, and the broader context that sets the rules and standards.

Twain’s Huck Finn has been given a new lease of life with Percival Everett’s novel James, which tells the story from the point of view of Huck’s Black co-traveller Jim. Clearly, without engaging with the original content, no matter how objectionable, we cannot think of alternative perspectives.

It’s perhaps fitting that we begin the new year with a cover story about working through conflict, and going beyond differences of opinion to discover understanding. We hope you continue to journey with Teacher Plus as we try to find new perspectives and new ways of engaging with teaching and learning in a world that is growing increasingly complex, and increasingly challenging.

We hope you have a great year ahead!
item.excerpt:encoded: Many of us may have grown up reading some of Mark Twain’s work – at least in abridged versions – and many of us may have taken delight in the stories, particularly in the rebellions of the central characters. Twain’s work has come under considerable criticism from many angles, but most notably for the use of racist terms and stereotypical depictions of Jim in The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn.

item.title: Books and beyond
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
For people who love books and reading, this is a happy time of year, being the season of literary festivals and book fairs in many parts of the country. While book fairs allow many small and regional language publishers to showcase their catalogues, literary festivals provide a forum for both seasoned and new authors to interact with audiences. One observer estimates that there are over 400 literary festivals held across India – suggesting this is not a phenomenon that is limited to big cities!

This year, I had the opportunity to visit the Hyderabad Book Fair and also participate in the Hyderabad Literary Festival (HLF), and even as I emerged from both with more books than I have shelf space for, I came away with a sense that all is well with the printed word even in this age of screen-immersion. At both events, I saw parents with young children in tow, teachers shepherding lines of uniform-clad pupils, young people lining up to get books signed by admired authors, and people of all ages lingering over stacks of poetry, fiction, and non-fiction. Between sessions at the HL, I noted children with their noses buried in a book, or taking part in one of the many additional activities that have now become an integral part of the event. These included storytelling, art and craft workshops, and stalls set up by scientific institutions that aimed to stoke curiosity and wonder and just possibly, nudge young people toward a career in science. There was much to stimulate thought and crafts to involve the hands.

We’ve written before in Teacher Plus about the value of engaging with the real world, about seeking and using opportunities to take children out of school and into the field. Granted, it’s not always easy to organize and does call for some commitment of resources, but it’s almost always worth the effort. For teachers too, such events can stoke new ideas and at the very least, point to new books for use in the classroom or for the library – I’m sure you’ll agree that browsing at the stalls beats staring at a printed catalogue anyday!

Our cover story this month takes on the vexing issue of the gender imbalance in STEM (science, technology, engineering and mathematics) education in India. Long-time contributor Simran Luthra examines the relatively low presence of women at higher levels in these fields, even though there has been a steady increase in the number of girls taking science and engineering courses. The notion of the “leaky pipeline” has been a matter of concern globally – why and where do the women drop off and what is the impact of their absence in these fields? Yet, there are women who inspire us with their staying power and dedication, as Divya Chowdary writes in a companion piece. What can we as teachers do to encourage girls to “stay with the [science] programme” as they move up the education ladder? Seeing more women at close quarters occupying leadership roles can be quite inspiring, allowing young girls to imagine the possibility. At HLF for instance, children were able to listen to Sowmya Swaminathan, Chief Scientist at the World Health Organization, and environmentalists like Janaki Lenin – and, even better, pick up their books!
item.excerpt:encoded: For people who love books and reading, this is a happy time of year, being the season of literary festivals and book fairs in many parts of the country. While book fairs allow many small and regional language publishers to showcase their catalogues, literary festivals provide a forum for both seasoned and new authors to interact with audiences.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Very true. I remember Dr Sowmya Swaminathan's mother; she was an educationist and a strong advocate of children's rights. In mid 90s, her NGO had released a video "Kuzhandaikku inda baaram thevaiyaa?"; this video explained the consequences of heavy bags carried by children to school and back. It was evidence based and was released with collaboration of Chennai DD. But unfortunately it was not implemented by schools (most of which were only interested in making money).

item.title: Text in context
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
For a few years now, our columnist Neerja Singh has been writing about how adults can stay “a step ahead” of the children we interact with, particularly in relation to digital tools and their use. Her column acknowledges the challenges of keeping up with accelerating changes in our environment, and the confusions that can arise when we see children occupying a world that is so different, in so many ways. While there’s no doubt that it is hard to find the right balance in parenting in these times, the teacher’s task poses particular difficulties. The teacher must not only keep up with modifications to curricula and shifts in pedagogic practice, but also be attuned to the way culture morphs from one generation to the next. And as one grows older, there is the feeling that you’re never quite going to be able to stay abreast, let alone get ahead of the changes. Yet, one must find a way to build bridges with the young, to find channels of communication that allow us to exchange our ideas, trading learning gathered by the adults from experience over years for that learning gathered by the young in the moment.

I recently asked my students to build a lexicon of their times, to list and explain new words that described their interactions with each other and the world, as well as actions they performed with their digital devices. In the space of half an hour, I acquired a vocabulary of several new words, from a new way of understanding “aura” to learning that one can “yank” text from one source and place it somewhere else. And of course that word that made it to this year’s Oxford English Dictionary Word of the Year – brain rot. This exercise reminded me of a piece of advice I had come across early in my teaching career, that one must always begin in the world of the child. What does the child know and how does the child interact with her immediate environment? This advice forces us also to acknowledge the child’s context of learning, and living, and attempt to make connections between the text (curricular material) and the context (the child’s life and surroundings). Starting my lesson by asking the students to draw on their own insights, developed through their own observations, led not only to engagement, but also to a sharper attention to their immediate environment – and to learn from this reflection.

Our cover theme for this issue pushes this idea further, taking it in two different directions: Nidhi Solanki talks about how we can bring the world into our classrooms, at different levels. And Subha Das Mollick offers a potted history on a very special set of readers that attempt to open up a child’s mind. There are many ways to help contextualize learning for the child, and we’d love to hear how our readers might approach this question, But whatever the approach, for the teacher, it can help not only go a step ahead, but more importantly, to stay in step with the child and her learning journey.
item.excerpt:encoded: For a few years now, our columnist Neerja Singh has been writing about how adults can stay “a step ahead” of the children we interact with, particularly in relation to digital tools and their use. Her column acknowledges the challenges of keeping up with accelerating changes in our environment, and the confusions that can arise when we see children occupying a world that is so different, in so many ways.

item.title: Growing comfortable with uncertainty
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
It’s almost a cliché that as one grows older, there are more questions than answers in one’s mind. We begin to accept that answers are transient, they shift and change with context and time. They are incomplete, with an uncertain edge and unclear tones. The deepest, most vexing questions are impossible to answer satisfactorily, defying neat responses. There are others that one cannot respond with more than “it depends”, a phrase that hangs in the air (or on the page/screen), invoking a mood like the start of a mystery novel.

Some time ago, I found many of the 20-somethings in my class restless and unable to focus on the tough text they had been asked to read that week, so I decided to ditch the discussion and instead open it up for a no-holds-barred conversation. It is placements season on my campus, and while a few had received offers of employment, the majority were looking anxiously at a hazy future. They were not sure if their degree would take them in the direction they had imagined; they were not sure if they had the skills the market demanded; and now, with AI entering the picture, they were not sure how long and to what extent they could hold the advantage of their education. As the adult in the room with some experience of that big world outside the shelter of the campus, they looked to me for answers, or at least some reassurance.

I was tempted to resort to comforting platitudes (“It will all work out.” “You’ll do fine.” “You’re all smart young people with a great future ahead of you.”). But even before I doled out the first one, I realized that wouldn’t do. They deserved honesty, not glibness. I needed to give them something to hold on to but also the assurance that they had – and could find – the internal resources to deal with challenges. It was also important that I first acknowledged their anxiety and recognized that their worries were legitimate, and attempt to share their view of the uncertainty facing them. Together, we had to learn to accept – perhaps even grow comfortable with – contingency and haziness, and to know that we’d never have a complete, defined, view of the future.

Facing uncertainty with younger students can be even tougher, as they may still think of the teacher as having all the answers. Indeed, they may come to depend on the idea that adults have certain knowledge of the world and can clearly see their way through life. Is there a way we can help young people get comfortable with uncertainty, and to not get overwhelmed when the path forward seems unclear? Can we model ways of saying “I don’t know” or “I’m not sure” while also emphasizing the need to constantly look for useful (not always complete) answers? How do you balance confidence and vulnerability? We’d love to hear your stories!
item.excerpt:encoded: It’s almost a cliché that as one grows older, there are more questions than answers in one’s mind. We begin to accept that answers are transient, they shift and change with context and time. They are incomplete, with an uncertain edge and unclear tones.

item.title: Take the break!
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
We’re entering that time of year when a certain lethargy takes over. Even as we stare at the piles of uncorrected examination papers, or pack away the last of the notes and materials from the year gone by, or are preparing for the coming onslaught of new minds, there is a sense that it is okay, for a week or two (or more if you’re lucky) when you can let those muscles relax and allow the brain to go a bit mushy. The heat doesn’t help. As I write this, Hyderabad is in the early 40s (degrees Celsius, in case you were wondering if I was on a nostalgia trip). Yes, there are mangoes and sugarcane juice, fresh avakkai and for a few short weeks, the succulent nungu, or ice apple. For some, there is the luxury of long, lazy summer days spent at home. Clearly, a recipe for inertia.

Lean into it, I’d say, and keep the guilt at bay. Easier said than done, of course.

There are still the many demands of life, professional and personal, as well as the need to catch up on all the things we had put off until we could find the time: the time that we should have at our disposal, now that we are officially on a break. But then, that is the nature of life; too much to do, and too little time to do it in.

Which brings me to the theme of our summer issue – geography. In the 17-odd years that Teacher Plus has been publishing subject-themed issues, we have covered geography before, but as with any theme we pick up, we find that there is always much more to be said, and not enough pages to say it in. So, we’ve come back to subjects and revisited them using new lenses, drawing on sensibilities of the times and bringing in new voices. As with every subject, there is also new knowledge that shifts what and how we teach. As a discipline, geography stands at the intersection of the physical or natural and the human sciences, and its study thus draws from physics and materials science as much as from sociology and political science. The new digital tools that we have at our disposal today offer the possibility of a much more intimate interaction with a range of geographic and geophysical phenomena. An understanding and appreciation of geography can inform how we think about and act on issues of climate change.

Through the month, we will bring you a range of articles on geography teaching – some inviting you to re-think the boundaries of the discipline, and others sharing ideas that have worked well in their classrooms. As always, there are unexpected lessons that emerge when one is attentive, in (perhaps) unlikely places – for instance, Sheel describes how a spiritual or religious event like the Mahakumbh mela presents an unmissable “concomitance of geography, history and culture”. On the other hand, Chandra Shekhar Balachandran exhorts us to see that geography is everywhere – and maybe in everything, all at once! But more on these and the other pieces that make up the issue in our regular weekly newsletters.

Long-time readers of the magazine will know that the subject special was earlier published as a double issue over May and June – a nice thick package of information and ideas from a wide range of contributors. With our digital avatar, the special issue unfolds over one month instead of two, but with a similar amount of content, as well as diversity. The geography teachers among you will have much to take into your classes in the new year, but there’s plenty in the issue that relates to other subjects as well, such as Tapasya Saha’s take on bringing current events into teaching, or Aditya Deshbandhu’s exhortation to make good use of video games for learning.

But even as we keep plying you with material through the year, we are conscious of the fact that the summer heat is not something we should take lightly, and that we also need to make good use of the break to recharge our batteries. How do you do that? We’d love to hear from you!
item.excerpt:encoded: We’re entering that time of year when a certain lethargy takes over. Even as we stare at the piles of uncorrected examination papers, or pack away the last of the notes and materials from the year gone by, or are preparing for the coming onslaught of new minds, there is a sense that it is okay, for a week or two (or more if you’re lucky) when you can let those muscles relax and allow the brain to go a bit mushy.

item.title: The tiny moments of joy
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
The monsoons arrived early this year, more than a week ahead of the expected landfall of June 1 in Kerala, according to the Indian Meteorological Bureau. From where I sit, in Hyderabad, this has meant overcast skies at a time in May when the heat is usually touching the low forties and we are still complaining. Suddenly, it seems like the summer has gone even before we have had enough time to grumble about it! So even though we have laid our ideas about geography to rest (for now) in the pages of our May issue, it’s clear that there’s always something to say about the weather. Maybe that’s something the British had right, in terms of conversation starters!

But weak jokes aside, once you begin paying attention to the little ways in which our surroundings shift and change, when trees flower and shed leaves, when kittens suddenly show up out of nowhere (as they have, in my garden), when the moon shades orange rather than yellow, and how bird calls change timbre, there’s no end to wonder. I find that every day when I am on my long drive to work, my eyes search through all the confusion of traffic for that patch of flaming gulmohur in the distance, or the brilliant yellow of the copper pod, and, if I’m lucky, the resplendence of the amaltas before they disappear after a too-brief flowering season. People often ask me how I can handle the distance and the tedious drive. Even as I acknowledge the extreme privilege of watching the world through the comfort of a car, I also know it is because I have learned how to focus on the beauty that is in the here and now, such as is available to me. Many years ago, when I had occasion to travel in the crowded local trains of Mumbai, I remember being astonished at how people were able to ignore the grime and the sweat and the congestion and do such things as play cards, string beans, or work on the crossword puzzle. Women would smile through their tiredness, exchanging jokes and catching up on gossip, even as their blouses turned dark with sweat.

There are some things we have little control over – weather and traffic, among others – and the only way to deal with them is to not pay them attention but instead to distract ourselves (or occupy ourselves) with the things that can bring us immediate joy.

It’s easy to dismiss this as a trite homily, but most of us will recognize the truth in triteness – we dismiss it not because it is untrue, but because we’ve heard it so often. But as we all know only too well (and we see in our classrooms every day), hearing does not always mean taking in, much less acting on. Maybe it’s time to start...with the tiniest joys?
item.excerpt:encoded: The monsoons arrived early this year, more than a week ahead of the expected landfall of June 1 in Kerala, according to the Indian Meteorological Bureau. From where I sit, in Hyderabad, this has meant overcast skies at a time in May when the heat is usually touching the low forties and we are still complaining.

item.title: What’s a vacation for, anyway?
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
Most schools around the country are a couple of weeks into the new term and already it must feel overwhelming! I know I feel that way when the academic year begins after the summer vacation; a month or five weeks is barely enough to catch your breath. Those who are not in the education system think that getting a regular vacation is such a great privilege that any complaints about workload are nothing more than an indulgence. I won’t deny that there used to be some truth to that. In the past, teachers may have had the luxury of switching off during vacation. They could actually turn to other, equally important obligations such as family, or even indulge in some real holiday activity for more than a rushed weekend. But these days, largely due to the Internet and our smart phones, it’s hard to completely switch off from the looming presence of work. Add to that the expectations that we “productively use” the holidays to catch up on reading, prepare materials, and update any number of records that schools are required to maintain for documentation.

But hard does not equal impossible. We must find a way to guard against the takeover of our life by work, to protect the little downtime we have – and that we are owed. Those breaks between school years, and that shorter but still much-needed pause between terms? By all means, use them to catch up on subject-related reading and to hone your teaching ideas, but prioritize the down time to recharge your batteries – emotional, psychological, physical. Let’s not forget that being an educator, while rewarding, can be extremely sapping on all these dimensions.

Speaking of the school calendar, we recently heard from Sathyanarayanan Mundayoor, an old friend of the magazine who runs the Lohit Youth Library Network in Arunachal Pradesh. Responding to our geography special, he writes:

“Since Teacher Plus May 2025 carried a Geography issue, an interesting aspect struck me: Has our school education system been considering geographical factors of our people & their lives, when the school calendar was/is evolved? Aren't we still following in many states, what the ruling power had found convenient to them? I see an exception in Assam – July being a rice plantation month in Assam, it is a month of Annual vacation, apart from 2 weeks during Bihu (Apr 15) and 2 weeks in Dassara (Navaratri). Also NE Himalayan states like Meghalaya and Nagaland schools have holidays in Dec & January – the winter & Christmas months.”

He believes it may be productive to re-think the suitability of our school calendar (particularly as imposed nationwide by central boards like the CBSE) for the varied geographic and cultural contexts of our country. We’d love to hear what you think about this, and what impact it has on how children, particularly those in rural areas, deal with school and examinations. How does extreme weather or the agricultural cycle affect school attendance, for instance?

We take the calendar for granted, as something that cannot be changed, something that just is. But is it?

Khair, as the Hindi-speakers say. Or whatever, as our children say. We plug on, but it is good to question the assumptions upon which our routines are based, don’t you think?
item.excerpt:encoded: Most schools around the country are a couple of weeks into the new term and already it must feel overwhelming! I know I feel that way when the academic year begins after the summer vacation; a month or five weeks is barely enough to catch your breath.

item.title: What makes a school safe?
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
The stream of disturbing incidents in educational institutions refuses to let up, with violence of various kinds that suggests a deep social malaise that cuts across socio-economic categories. Those in charge of policy making and regulation respond in predictable ways, issuing orders that ramp up surveillance and punitive measures. The first takes the form of increasing securitization of schools, while the second calls for stringent action after an incident. The CBSE’s latest mandate requires all affiliated schools to install high-resolution CCTV cameras and maintain recordings for at least two weeks. These cameras are to be installed at gates, in corridors, classrooms, libraries, laboratories, playgrounds and other common areas, with washrooms exempted for reasons of privacy. In effect, there will be eyes on members of the school community almost everywhere within the premises.

There’s no doubt that surveillance cameras have helped apprehend wrongdoers and clarify the circumstances of a crime, or a troubling incident that may not count technically as a crime. There’s also an argument that the knowledge of surveillance can deter potential wrongdoers. This argument also extends to the application of severe punitive measures when crimes are committed – so the presence of the death penalty is considered a major deterrent for what a society considers “heinous crimes”. But we know only too well that the existence of stringent laws has not made things safer for vulnerable groups, often because the implementation of those laws is lax, or there are technical loopholes that allow perpetrators to go free. Moreover, the wheels of justice, as they say, move exceedingly slow, and the process can often traumatize the survivor. Surveillance systems, too, can end up being misused to impinge on privacy and freedom – would any of us want to be watched all the time, even if we’re not doing anything wrong?

So how do we make our educational spaces safer, and education itself a more nurturing experience? It is partly about having rules and reasonable security measures in place, no doubt. But a sense of safety doesn’t come only from sturdy walls and strict rules; it should also be about being safe in other, less tangible ways. Safe to speak our mind. Safe to experiment. Safe to explore and discover. Safe to make mistakes and learn from them. A school’s safety agenda should include these protections as well, for all children.

It’s a tall order, no doubt. But if schools can’t be safe spaces in all these ways, then what place can?
item.excerpt:encoded: The stream of disturbing incidents in educational institutions refuses to let up, with violence of various kinds that suggests a deep social malaise that cuts across socio-economic categories. Those in charge of policy making and regulation respond in predictable ways, issuing orders that ramp up surveillance and punitive measures.

item.title: If you can think it...
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
“Future thinking” is a term that’s often employed these days in policy circles, think tanks, and research laboratories. It’s also used a lot in the technology world, when imagining new tools for emergent needs – or needs that have not yet been felt. Sometimes referred to as “futures thinking” (in the plural), it can take a systematic and rule-based approach to construct scenarios and build trajectories to achieve them or change them. It requires imagination and the ability to speculate, but also the skill of seeing pathways and how to achieve the desired future, or in some cases, how to avoid a negative scenario that seems to be looming. Climate change activists and environmentalists for instance try to think about what would happen if we continued destroying our habitat at our current rate, and then work backwards to figure out how we might avoid or better manage, a catastrophe.

But wouldn’t you agree that teaching is – or should be – a project of making the future? Day after day in our classrooms, we are working with young minds so that they can be ready for the world when they step out into it. Even when we are dealing with subjects that explain the world as it is, or as it has come to be, the point is to equip them with the understanding and skills that will help them occupy that world and remake it in better ways. The classroom is, of course, embedded within a complex and ever-changing ecology, and thinking about the future cannot disregard the problems of our present. Ultimately, it’s a careful combination of recognizing where we are (pragmatism), seeing where we want to go (idealism), and thinking about how we get there, given our strengths and challenges (strategy). So, for us as teachers, it involves staying in touch with what is happening in our respective fields and in society, and making our teaching relevant in ways that can serve children both the present and the future. Yes, there is exam-directed work, but there also needs to be skilling and mindset building for resilience and adaptability. I find that it’s useful, now and then, to stop what we are doing and just loosen our minds with what is often called “blue sky thinking”. Ask yourself, what is the kind of future I want? Allow yourself to fantasize about this perfect world, indulging in painting the smallest details. Then pull back and think about what needs to be done in the present to achieve at least some part of it. Is there something here that can trickle down to what and how you teach?

But for now: Happy Teacher’s Day!
item.excerpt:encoded: “Future thinking” is a term that’s often employed these days in policy circles, think tanks, and research laboratories. It’s also used a lot in the technology world, when imagining new tools for emergent needs – or needs that have not yet been felt.

item.title: The meaning of ritual
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
The festival season is upon us and it’s hard to escape the hard sell from commercial establishments of all kinds – buy this, buy that, dhamaka offer this, special discount that. On the one hand, it’s touted to be a time when we reflect with gratitude for what we have, and take pleasure in our social bonds and cultural heritage. But on the other, it’s about giving in to our material desires, whether for simpler things like clothes and trinkets, or for the big budget items like jewellery and cars. Somewhere lost in all of this is the festival itself – what it represents, what it celebrates, and what it means.

But then, within the sanctuary of our homes and our families and communities, if we block out the aggressive marketing, there are ways in which we can rediscover that essence. We clean our homes, we light lamps, we share sweets with neighbours, and we spend time with family. The religious among us may perform traditional acts of worship or visit temples. Personally, I don’t generally set much store by ritual, and I recall as a child I almost always rebelled against doing something simply because “it has always been done” or because “it is good to do it”. The resistance came from not being given the reason why something had to be done, why it had become a practice, and why it was still relevant to my life. But as I’ve grown older, I have come to understand that some rituals do make sense, that they are not “empty” acts of habit but can provide a structure that helps contain other, necessary activities. So much of our everyday depends on habitual actions that can be dismissed as mere ritual. But such rituals can help us focus on the moment in a way that can be meaningful.

Take, for instance, morning assembly in school. We’re asked to stand in line, follow a pattern of behaviour, participate in some shared commitments – national anthem, school pledge, etc. Even if we disagree with the content of some of these “mantras”, their performance marks the beginning of the school day, a passage from home life to a period of learning. Ringing the bell is a ritual that marks the separation of one unit of learning from the next, giving students a signal that it’s time to shift gears.

Holiday rituals can sometimes become empty performances, but they also hold the possibility of recovering meaning. This may be why, even as I continue to question the relevance of some rituals, I am reluctant to give them up, sometimes, for purely aesthetic reasons. Who can, after all, deny the beauty of oil lamps lining a balcony ledge on Deepavali, the harmony of carols sung at Christmas, the aroma and flavour of sheer khorma at Eid, or the colourful kolam patterns on wet earth at Sankranti? These are rituals too, but in the doing of them we discover something, we feel something.
item.excerpt:encoded: The festival season is upon us and it’s hard to escape the hard sell from commercial establishments of all kinds – buy this, buy that, dhamaka offer this, special discount that.

item.title: The rewards come later
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
If there’s one thing teachers must learn to accept as part of the job, it’s delayed gratification. And maybe that’s too big a word to use for the little rewards that we come by, days, months, and even years later. But when those moments come, they do bring a warm glow to the heart and make up for the hours of what seems at times to be the routine drudgery of our everyday lives.

Many years ago, fuelled by a sense of total irritation with a group of students who had failed to take a deadline seriously, I dashed off (what I thought was) a sharply written note telling them what awaited them in the “real world” where such tardiness would not be excused nor tolerated, and warning them about the consequences of not attending to detail in substance and presentation. My general exasperation with their casual attitude to class assignments led me to throw in some general advice about the uncompromising nature of workplace expectations. I hit “send” on the email and waited, expecting some contrite apologies to trickle in. Even 24 hours later, not one from the group of 23 had acknowledged the email, let alone responded to it with something like an apology. Youngsters these days, I thought to myself, and put the incident behind me.

Five years later, a note popped into my inbox, from a sender whose name seemed familiar, but whose address looked associated with a distant foreign university. I opened it, anticipating a request for a letter of recommendation or some such. But no, it was a response to that email from all those years ago. This former student had just got a teaching job at a university overseas, and for some strange reason he decided to revisit that email from me. Reading it, he said, was like going through an orientation session before he embarked on his first job, and he was finally able to understand and see the relevance of what I had wanted to convey.

Often in the pages of Teacher Plus (or now, as you school through the website) we find accounts from individuals – some of them teachers – of how they went in a certain direction based on what they had heard from a teacher in primary school. Or how a lesson that went over their heads in a math class suddenly made sense years later, when they were in a situation where the principle was applied. Our effectiveness as teachers is almost always measured by performance in examinations, but we know that marks are not the best indicator of understanding, and that often takes years to gauge – and usually, we are not around to see the penny drop. It’s rare that we even hear directly about whether and how we have impacted learning. But when we do, the warmth of satisfaction is palpable.
item.excerpt:encoded: If there’s one thing teachers must learn to accept as part of the job, it’s delayed gratification. And maybe that’s too big a word to use for the little rewards that we come by, days, months, and even years later.

item.title: Museums as gateways to experience
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
As teachers, we are constantly on a mission to create opportunities to stoke and nurture a sense of curiosity and wonder in our students, to get them to think beyond the mundane, to make connections across time, location, and domains, and open their minds to a variety of perspectives. Each of us is, to a large extent, trapped inside the bubbles of our own experience, our thinking structured by what we have been exposed to. Books offer one way of encountering new worlds, of inhabiting other lives, or looking under the surface of events and phenomena. But books demand an exercise of the imagination, a willingness to disconnect from the present, the material reality we inhabit, and lose ourselves in the story. It’s a shift that not everyone can make easily.

Which is how museums enter the picture. Big or small, thematic or general, museums are deliberately structured to expose one to material artefacts, or sensory experiences, that force us to make that shift. Some museums house objects of various kinds, while others construct aural, visual, tactile, or otherwise immersive experiences.

For me, museums have been like portals, as Divya Choudhary describes them. They offer an entry into a space of discovery, along the way unsettling long-held beliefs. I’ve spent hours wandering the halls of museums of natural history, awed by reconstructed skeletons and massive dinosaur bones, or hunched over glass-topped tables holding exquisitely fashioned glass flowers. I’ve also come away sobered by the violence of war, or transported by the texture of the brushstrokes on a canvas that the familiar textbook image bears no comparison to. Museums force a confrontation – with history, with nature, with technology, with ideas. They are places of memory and memorialization, but they also can become catalysts for re-thinking, as Sita Reddy notes, in her piece about the Flora Indica exhibit at the famous Kew Gardens in the UK.

When we considered doing a special issue on museums, we suspected it would be a challenge to find people who could tease out the many ways in which the topic could be explored, and related to school education, but as always, the breadth and generosity of the contributors more than addressed our apprehensions. The articles in this issue talk about museums through multiple lenses – from the process and politics of curation to how collections can be interpreted. They also talk about spaces that serve the same function as museums – the ambitious Mapusa Mogi mural, for instance, or even the traces of history we may glimpse in our old cities. The articles take us into the often invisible dynamics that go into the making of museums – the process of archiving, the assignment of ownership, the politics of labelling.

And as with every topic we begin to explore, we find the possibilities are endless, for the pleasure of learning, both for the classroom and for life. And the icing on the cake? You get to hold a rare printed issue of the magazine with all the beautiful illustrations and photographs that such a theme brings with it!
item.excerpt:encoded: As teachers, we are constantly on a mission to create opportunities to stoke and nurture a sense of curiosity and wonder in our students, to get them to think beyond the mundane, to make connections across time, location, and domains, and open their minds to a variety of perspectives.

item.title: The devil is in the detail
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
I’ve been a writing teacher for the better part of three decades, and one of the things I repeat ad infinitum (and possibly, nauseum, for the listeners) is that they must (absolutely must) check their copy carefully before submitting. It’s the misused apostrophes and misplaced commas, the unnecessary capitals and the missing full stops, and of course, the run-on text without appropriate paragraphing that make me groan. I know in most cases it is not a lack of knowledge, but just a rush to be done with an assignment, to get past the task. These quibbles are seen as a mark of an obsessive mind, generally. I tell my students that the consequences of not paying attention to what they see as “tiny quibbles” will become evident when they apply for a job. If those recruiters are anything like me, they will immediately distrust the mind that missed those details.

A former boss dubbed it the “not quite right” factor – NQR, for short. We build beautiful edifices, with marble stairways and high ceilings, but in a corner somewhere will be a bit of fallen plaster, a chipped tile, or, most familiarly, a sign hung crookedly. We pay for certain clothing brands because of what we call “the finish” and what designers and creators call “the detailing”: smooth edges and perfectly folded hems, no loose threads anywhere. We appreciate the little touches in restaurants, hotels, and other service establishments that make us willing to pay just a bit more. We know, instinctively, that when the small things are attended to so painstakingly, the big things are likely to be well sorted out.

Of course, detail can sometimes be cosmetic, just a veneer that hides either shallowness or even absence of something substantive and real. But one can usually tell the difference between an attention to detail that comes from real depth and care, and that which is just decoration. When someone takes pride in their work, they tend to be uncompromising about the quality of their output – it has to be the best they can do. Writer Ann Patchett once said in an interview that she doesn’t stop working on a novel until she is satisfied that it is the best book she can write. Similarly, mathematicians aim for proofs that demonstrate “elegance” and “parsimony”, that is, explanations that are clean and clear, with nothing that is redundant.

One can find any number of examples from every field that emphasize the need for care and focus, and the cover theme of this month’s issue illustrates this by unpacking how to build such an attitude in children. If attention to detail, and the focus it demands, becomes a habit, then it is not just the veneer; it is something that runs through everything one does.
item.excerpt:encoded: I’ve been a writing teacher for the better part of three decades, and one of the things I repeat ad infinitum (and possibly, nauseum, for the listeners) is that they must (absolutely must) check their copy carefully before submitting.

item.title: What we miss when we make it easy
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
From the end of the year to the beginning of the next, across the country, it’s the season of literary and cultural festivals. From the celebration of music and dance that is the Marghazhi Vizha of Chennai to the carefully curated assemblage of art that is the Kochi Biennale, and the scores of literary gatherings in cities as far apart as Jaipur and Kolkata, Patna and Hyderabad, Shillong and Mumbai, there’s no dearth of opportunities to travel through the cultural landscape, no matter what your taste.
 
Most literary and arts festivals have something for children too: author talks, art workshops, and interactive storytelling, for instance. So, one increasingly sees whole families make an outing of it, giving children the opportunity to browse, listen to people talk about books and reading, and engage in non-routine activities that are at once fun and instructive. At the Hyderabad Literary Festival, for instance, I saw a child pick up a book of poetry and approach the author to sign her copy, and another proudly wave around a cartoon he had drawn. Some sat in sessions, quietly listening, while others walked through exhibitions and art installations with wide eyes, not quite understanding, perhaps, but taking it all in. Among the many things literary and cultural festivals can do, one is to spark curiosity and see where the pursuit of an idea can lead. Children watch and listen to successful people who have followed their curiosity and turned it into art and story, they see how sticking to a passion or making a commitment to one’s ideals can take you places, and possibly, they get a sense of the work that goes into it all.
 
There’s a short poem doing the rounds on social media, titled For a student who used AI to write a paper that asks the question, “what are you trying to be free of?” and ends with the line: “Love is for the ones who love the work.” It’s something many of us teachers have wondered about: what exactly are kids doing with the time they purportedly save by taking technology-enabled shortcuts? This was the question that led us to the theme of this month’s cover story – why has hard work now become so difficult to commit to? And what are we missing in the process of learning when we are forced to make everything easy? Why should engagement be the burden a teacher must carry, rather than something that is fostered in the learner, driven by an energy that they bring to the task? These are the questions that Mahak Khanna puzzles through in her article, questions that we have all asked so often, more so in recent times, when we have needed to always think about ways to harness and hold children’s attention.
 
Taking children to events such as literary and cultural festivals may be one way to get them to re-focus, in a non-instrumental way, on the joy and pain of creation, prompting the understanding that it is the tough work-out that builds muscle, that it is the revisions that make the masterpiece. That shortcuts may make things easy, but they rarely make things shine.
item.excerpt:encoded: From the end of the year to the beginning of the next, across the country, it’s the season of literary and cultural festivals. From the celebration of music and dance that is the Marghazhi Vizha of Chennai to the carefully curated assemblage of art that is the Kochi Biennale, and the scores of literary gatherings in cities...

item.title: Facing AI anxiety
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
Unless you have chosen to stay away from the news and have silenced all the streams of chatter that occupy your phone and other communication channels, it has been practically impossible to ignore the buzz around artificial intelligence. This reached a crescendo during recent weeks, with India hosting the Global AI Summit in New Delhi – which, from all accounts, was quite a mela. All the big names in the tech industry were there, as were all those who are trying to push back against the hype. The mood was largely celebratory, the air filled with excitement and expectation, and the week peppered with multiple photo-ops and snappy bites from industry leaders. The “New Delhi Declaration on AI” was announced, endorsed by around 88 countries, which pledged to support responsible and inclusive AI systems that would benefit society.

I don’t blame you if your eyes are beginning to glaze over and your mind not-so-slowly turning off, recognizing in these words a message one has heard before, from the early age of computers, then the internet and mobile connectivity, riding on the promise of social media, and now, artificial intelligence. We have been inundated with memes and reels that poke fun at our dependence on these technologies while also reinforcing the sense that the machines will soon open up a wonderful new world. We’re left not really knowing what or how to think. Is life going to be better or is it going to get worse? Who will benefit (apart from the companies themselves) and who will be left out? Is the government moving fast enough to regulate use of AI or, in industry parlance, “put guard rails around it”, or is it going to be a free for all, with the technology racing ahead before we can respond with regulation?

It’s really hard to know how to think about it, and how to prepare ourselves for this new world. A parent recently asked me, “How do we AI-proof our children’s futures?” In other words, how do we take advantage of these shiny new tools, and how do we make sure we are not being taken advantage of?

At the edges of the Global AI Summit were groups raising some of the critical issues that regulators need to be thinking of, arguing that we need strong limitations on how and where AI should be used, asking governments to pay attention to the risks for children and other vulnerable groups, and calling for stronger regulations on data capture. Some groups went so far as to say that there should be spaces and aspects of life where AI is not allowed free rein. But those of us who work with children, and are in the job of building thinking individuals, cannot wait for regulation. Instead, we need to protect these spaces of learning and use them to build critical capacities that will weather an AI-suffused world.

And just how do we do that? Teacher Plus has been grappling with this question in different ways. Our columnist Neerja Singh has consistently been looking for ways that teachers and parents can stay “a step ahead” and in this month’s cover story, Kamakshi Balasubramanian speaks of how we can help children become thoughtful information consumers. We’ll keep looking for ways to challenge (and make use of) new technological incursions into education, and we hope you will share your ideas too.
item.excerpt:encoded: Unless you have chosen to stay away from the news and have silenced all the streams of chatter that occupy your phone and other communication channels, it has been practically impossible to ignore the buzz around artificial intelligence.
